analogy: Think of "Role of the Government in Health" through the chapter's own opening images: 10 Social and Political Life Chapter 1: On Equality UNIT TWO 11 Teachers note These two chapters (Chapters 2 and 3) on State Government are an attempt to discuss the functions and structure of government through concrete situations. We have chosen health as an example;... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 10 Social and Political Life Chapter 1: On Equality, 2CHAPTER, 16 Social and Political Life, THE COST OF A CURE. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Role of the Government in Health is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 10 Social and Political Life Chapter 1: On Equality UNIT TWO

The text explains that 11 Teachers note These two chapters (Chapters 2 and 3) on State Government are an attempt to discuss the functions and structure of government through concrete situations.

The text explains that we have chosen health as an example; there could have been other, equally important, choices.

The text explains that chapter 2 discusses health as an important issue for people.

The text explains that there are both public and private aspects of health provisioning.

The text explains that while the Constitution supports a view that the right to health is an aspect of our fundamental rights, its provisioning is rather unequal.

The text explains that through the accounts provided, learners will begin to visualise the ideal or desired role of government, and the meanings behind its structures.

The text explains that some ways in which this situation can be changed are also discussed.

The text explains that chapter 3 focuses on how the government functions, and discusses ideas of representation, accountability and public welfare.

The text explains that though both the executive and the legislature are presented, one should not expect students to retain fine distinctions.

The text explains that it would be best to patiently encourage them to ask questions such as, Who is the most powerful person?, Why cant the MLA solve the problem?, etc.

The text explains that such queries will enable them to construct a sense of the government apparatus.

The text explains that it is important that learners acquire the confidence to express their views on public issues and understand the role of government through the exercises given in the chapters.

The text explains that you could choose familiar issues such as water, transport, school- fees, books, child-labour, etc., for them to discuss and arrive at how these problems need to be tackled.

The text explains that allow them to express these ideas through wall charts.

The text explains that given that discussions on the government and its functioning often lead to boredom and cynicism, we need to be able to make the classroom session less didactic and more interactive while teaching these lessons.

The text explains that 12 2CHAPTER In a democracy people expect the government to work for their welfare.

The text explains that in this chapter we shall examine the meanings and problems related to health.

The text explains that in what ways do you think this topic is related to the work of government?

The text explains that 15 Patients usually have to wait in long queues in government hospitals, like this one.

The text explains that the country also has considerable experience and knowledge in running a public healthcare system.

The text explains that this is a system of hospitals and health centres run by the government.

The text explains that in this chapter, we will look at some of the reasons for this.

The text explains that what problems did Aman face in the government hospital?

The text explains that public health services The public health service is a chain of health centres and hospitals run by the government.

The text explains that large cities have many government hospitals such as the one where Aman was taken and also specialised government hospitals.

The text explains that the health service is called public for many reasons.

The text explains that in order to fulfil its commitment of providing health care to all citizens, the government has established these hospitals and health centres.

The text explains that also, the resources needed to run these services are obtained from the money that we, the public, pay to the government as taxes.

The text explains that one of the most important aspects of the public health system is that it is meant to provide quality health care services either free or at a low cost, so that even the poor can seek treatment.

The text explains that another important function of public health is to take action to prevent the spread of diseases such as TB, malaria, jaundice, cholera, Why pay taxes to the government?

The text explains that government uses tax money for providing many public services for the benefit of all citizens.

The text explains that some services such as defence, police, judicial system, highways etc. benefit all citizens.

The text explains that tax money is utilised for relief and rehabilitation in case of natural disasters such as floods, earthquakes, tsunami etc.

The text explains that government provides some services especially for the poor who cannot afford to purchase them from the market.

The text explains that 19 In what ways is the public health system meant for everyone?

The text explains that this has to be organised by the government with the participation of people otherwise it is not effective.

The text explains that according to our Constitution, it is the primary duty of the government to ensure the welfare of the people and provide health care facilities to all.

The text explains that the government must safeguard the Right to Life of every person.

The text explains that the Court also said that it was the duty of the government to provide the necessary health services, including treatment in emergency situations.

The text explains that therefore, the Court asked the State Government to give him the money that he had spent on his treatment.

The text explains that as the name suggests, private health facilities are not owned or controlled by the government.

The text explains that this includes schools, hospitals, telephone services, etc.

The text explains that people can demand these services and also raise questions about their non-functioning.

The text explains that private: An activity or service that is organised by an individual or company for their own gain.

The text explains that medical tourists: This refers to foreigners who come to this country specifically for medical treatment at hospitals that offer worldclass facilities at a lower cost than what they would have to pay in their own countries.

The text explains that communicable diseases: These are diseases that are spread from one person to another in many ways such as through water, food , air, etc.

The text explains that oPD: This is the short form for Out Patient Department.

The text explains that this is where people are first brought in and treated in a hospital without being admitted to any special ward.

The text explains that ethics: Moral principles that influence a persons behaviour Generic names: These are chemical names of the drugs.

The text explains that for example, acetyl salicylic acid is the generic name of Aspirin.

The text explains that facility Affordability Availability Quality Private Public Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) www.in.undp.org

Source: NCERT Class 7 Social and Political Life II, Chapter 2: Role of the Government in Health

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
